subtopics.title: Inference, Deduction and Evaluation
common_misconceptions: The writer's opinion is always correct
Literal comprehension is sufficient for SS2
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rule: Inference = conclusion drawn from evidence in text + background knowledge. Deduction = logical conclusion that MUST be true given the evidence. Evaluation = judging the quality/validity of arguments. Tone indicators: sarcastic ('Oh great'), critical ('unfortunately'), approving ('remarkably'). Practice with editorials from Nigerian newspapers (The Guardian, Vanguard, Punch).

subtopics.title: Argumentative Essay
common_misconceptions: Only one side should be presented
Emotional appeals are stronger than evidence
subtopics.teaching_note: Structure: Introduction (hook + background + thesis statement) → Body paragraphs (point + evidence + explanation) → Counter-argument + refutation → Conclusion (restate thesis + call to action). Evidence types: statistics, expert testimony, examples, analogies. Nigerian topics: 'Education is better than money', 'Social media does more harm than good', 'Federal character principle promotes mediocrity'. Avoid fallacies: ad hominem, straw man, false dilemma.

subtopics.title: Expository Essay
subtopics.teaching_note: Expository = explain, inform, describe. No personal opinion. Patterns: cause-effect (why something happened), compare-contrast (similarities/differences), process (how-to steps), definition (extended definition with examples). Thesis: clear statement of what will be explained. Body: topic sentences, transitions (furthermore, however, consequently). Nigerian topics: 'Causes of unemployment in Nigeria', 'How to prepare for WAEC', 'The effects of climate change on agriculture'.

subtopics.title: Formal Report Writing
common_misconceptions: A report is the same as an essay
First person is acceptable in formal reports (use passive voice instead)
subtopics.teaching_note: Format: Title → Terms of Reference (who asked, what for) → Procedure (how information was gathered) → Findings (what was discovered, use headings/subheadings) → Conclusion (summary of findings) → Recommendations (specific, actionable suggestions). Use passive voice: 'It was observed that...' not 'I observed that...'. Use formal language, avoid contractions. Numbered sections improve readability. Nigerian context: accident reports, inspection reports, project reports.

subtopics.title: Reported Speech
common_misconceptions: Tense always changes in reported speech (no change if reporting something still true)
Say and tell are used the same way
Question marks are used in reported questions
subtopics.teaching_note: Grammar rules: Present → Past, Past → Past Perfect, will → would, can → could, may → might. 'Say' doesn't take indirect object: 'He said that...'. 'Tell' takes indirect object: 'He told me that...'. Reported questions: word order becomes statement form, no question mark. 'Where are you going?' → He asked where I was going. 'Are you coming?' → He asked if/whether I was coming. Time: now → then, today → that day, yesterday → the previous day, tomorrow → the following day.

subtopics.title: Conditional Sentences
common_misconceptions: Second conditional uses 'was' for all subjects (use 'were' for all in formal English)
Third conditional refers to future events
Will can be used in if-clause (NO in standard conditionals)
subtopics.teaching_note: Zero: If + present simple, present simple (general truths). 'If you heat water, it boils.' First: If + present simple, will + infinitive (real future). 'If it rains, I will stay home.' Second: If + past simple, would + infinitive (unreal present/future). 'If I were rich, I would travel.' Third: If + past perfect, would have + past participle (unreal past). 'If I had studied, I would have passed.' Mixed: If + past perfect, would + infinitive (past condition, present result). 'If I had studied medicine, I would be a doctor now.'
